explicit_text: My grandfather, Arthur N. Reed, settled at Old Mulberry Grove Plantation during the latter part of the War between the States. He was originally from Connecticut and operated both a bank and a mercantile establishment, though I never knew exactly where the latter was located. When Federal gunboats threatened Jacksonville in 1862, he moved his family to the plantation for safety. He was a kind man who cared deeply for his family and those who worked on the plantation.

explicit_text: The War between the States was a dangerous time for my family, especially around 1862 when Federal gunboats threatened Jacksonville's harbor. My grandfather, Arthur N. Reed, settled at Old Mulberry Grove Plantation during the latter part of this war to keep the family safe from bombardment. It was a tense period, and the plantation became a refuge away from the war zone.

explicit_text: Both my grandfather, Arthur N. Reed, and my grandmother Harriet Douglas Reed's father, ZUdlie Thomas Douglas, were natives of Connecticut. My great-grandfather Douglas moved from Connecticut to St. Augustine in 1826 and later became a judge on the Supreme Court of Florida. Connecticut was the original home of my family before they settled in Florida.

explicit_text: My grandmother, Harriet Douglas Reed, was the daughter of ZUdlie Thomas Douglas, a Connecticut native who settled in St. Augustine in 1826. She lived at Old Mulberry Grove Plantation with my grandfather, Arthur N. Reed, and was cared for in her old age by Maria Lyles, her personal maid. Harriet was part of the family that helped establish our roots in Florida.

explicit_text: My great-grandfather, ZUdlie Thomas Douglas, was a native of Connecticut who settled in St. Augustine in 1826. That same year, he was appointed as a Judge of the Supreme Court of Florida by President John Quincy Adams and served for nineteen years, being reappointed by three successive presidents. He played an important role in Florida's early judicial history.

explicit_text: St. Augustine, Florida, is where my great-grandfather ZUdlie Thomas Douglas settled in 1826 after leaving Connecticut. It was also the place where my family’s roots in Florida began. The silk-worm industry was started there many years ago, and remnants like the mulberry trees at Old Mulberry Grove Plantation remind me of that history.

explicit_text: Jacksonville was the nearby city to Old Mulberry Grove Plantation where my family lived until about age eight. During the Civil War in 1862, Federal gunboats arrived in Jacksonville’s harbor, which prompted my grandfather to move the family to the plantation for safety. Later, my brother Reed Pearson moved back to Jacksonville after inheriting the plantation.
implicit_text: This nearby city to Old Mulberry Grove Plantation was where my family lived until about age eight. During the Civil War in 1862, Federal gunboats arrived in its harbor, which prompted my grandfather to move the family to the plantation for safety. Later, my brother moved back to this city after inheriting the plantation.

explicit_text: The Old Mulberry Grove Plantation faced east along the St. Johns River, which runs almost due south to north in that area. The river was a vital part of our lives, with a long dock where steamboats like the Crescent City and Mary Draper would tie up. My grandfather even kept two rowboats, the John Perry and Fanny Perry, which he used to travel to Jacksonville.

explicit_text: I spent my early childhood at Old Mulberry Grove Plantation, a grand old place with a large white two-story house facing the St. Johns River. My grandfather, Arthur N. Reed, settled there during the Civil War to keep the family safe from the fighting in Jacksonville. The plantation had extensive grounds, a big house with a library, gardens, and a dock where steamboats stopped. It was a lively place with many workers and family members living and working there, and I have many fond memories of growing up there.

It was a lively place with many workers and family members living and working there, and I have many fond memories of growing up there.

explicit_text: The Crescent City was one of the steamboats that regularly tied up at the dock of Old Mulberry Grove Plantation on the St. Johns River. River traffic was heavy then, and these boats would deliver freight and food orders to the plantation. I remember watching these boats come and go as part of everyday life on the river.

explicit_text: The Mary Draper was another steamboat that frequently stopped at our plantation dock on the St. Johns River. Along with other boats like the Crescent City and Manatee, it helped bring supplies and freight to Old Mulberry Grove Plantation. These boats were an important link between our home and the outside world.

explicit_text: The Manatee was one of the steamboats that came to Old Mulberry Grove Plantation’s dock on the St. Johns River. It was part of the busy river traffic that supplied the plantation with food and other goods. I recall the excitement of seeing these boats arrive and knowing they brought what we needed.

explicit_text: The Three Friends was a steamboat that sometimes tied up at our plantation dock. I remember when I was about eight years old, my brother Reed Pearson let me blow the whistle of the Three Friends. It was a thrilling moment, though the rope came down with a loud clanging noise that startled me. It was one of the memorable experiences of my childhood at Old Mulberry Grove Plantation.

explicit_text: My grandfather kept the John Perry, a four-oared rowboat, in the boathouse under the docks at Old Mulberry Grove Plantation. He used it to travel to Jacksonville, which was nearby. The boat was part of the daily life on the river and essential for getting around before telephones and modern transport.

explicit_text: Near the Old Mulberry Grove Plantation was a large Indian camp and burying ground where a representative of the Smithsonian Institution conducted excavations. They found twenty-four large skeletons arranged in a circle around an old fireplace deep underground. This archaeological work connected our land to a much older history and was fascinating to me as a child.

This archaeological work connected our land to a much older history and was fascinating to me as a child.
